title: Years of schooling have improved significantly over time
source: Barro-Lee Dataset. Barro, Robert and Jong-Wha Lee, 2013, “A New Data Set of Educational Attainment in the World, 1950-2010.” Journal of Development Economics, vol 104, pp.184-198.
visdescription: The average number of years of schooling has expanded dramatically. The data is presented as a line chart showing increases in the number of years of schooling.

scenes.text: In 1920, the average adult had only 1.6 years of education—ranging from nearly zero years in Mali to seven in the United States.

By 1960, the global average had doubled to 3.5 years, but the pace of progress was slow. On average, the world population gained half a year of school per decade. In low-income countries like Mali, there was almost no improvement .
…but trends changed dramatically after 1960. Many countries will make rapid progress in improving years of schooling, while some are left with persistently low levels of education.

Between 1960 and 2025, the average in the Republic of Korea jumped from around 3.5 to more than 14 years. Egypt improved from less than one year to more than eight years of schooling. In India, it went from around one to nearly eight years, and in Mexico from 2.3 to more than 10 years over the same period.

By 2025, the global average for 25–64-year-olds was more than nine years, equivalent to a lower secondary education. But the gaps between countries are large: the average in Mali is around 3 years, compared to 14 years in Korea and Ireland.
